title: Chapter 56: Confrontation
content: Glass and sky.

Vance's office occupied the entire sixtieth floor — a cathedral of glass and steel overlooking a city that sprawled in every direction like a circuit board. The walls were floor-to-ceiling windows. No blinds. No curtains. Nothing between the occupant and the world below except sixty floors of empty air.

The city lights smeared across the glass like wet paint. Wren could smell the ozone from the building's climate system, the leather of the furniture, the faint chemical trace of the Counter-Signal prototype.
Vance stood in the center of the room. Alone.

She'd sent her security away — Wren had watched them go through the building's cameras, retreating down the stairwell with the confused obedience of people who'd been ordered to leave their principal unprotected. Vance had dismissed them. Chosen to face this by herself.
That was either courage or arrogance. With Vance, it was always impossible to tell.

She was smaller than Wren had imagined. Shorter. Thinner. Her silver hair was pulled back tight, and she wore a charcoal dress that probably cost more than Wren's mother made in a month. But what struck Wren most was her stillness. No fear. No tension. She stood like a surgeon before a routine procedure — focused, confident, utterly certain of her own competence.
In her hand, the Counter-Signal prototype pulsed with light.

Small. Elegant. The size of a phone, its surface smooth and dark, with a single amber light that throbbed in a rhythm Wren recognized. The glitch frequency, inverted. A mirror image of everything she was.
"You came," Vance said. Not a question. An observation. Her voice was warm and precise, like a cello string tuned to concert pitch.
Wren stepped forward. Her footsteps made no sound on the polished floor.
"Subject 13." Vance tilted her head. "The one who exceeded all projections. Do you have any idea how rare that is?"

Wren said nothing. The glass walls hummed faintly with wind pressure from outside. She could feel the building's systems at the edge of her awareness — still compromised, still hers, a weapon she could deploy with a thought.

"I've reviewed your file more times than I can count," Vance continued. She began to pace, slowly, circling toward the window. The city lights caught the planes of her face, casting shadows that shifted like water. "Your response to ANX-00 was anomalous from the beginning. Faster onset. Deeper integration. The invisibility should have plateaued at Stage 3. Instead, you progressed through stages we didn't even know existed."
"You drugged me without my consent."

"I gave you an opportunity." Vance's voice carried no defensiveness. Only curiosity. "Do you know how rare it is to create something that surpasses its creator? Every artist, every scientist, every parent dreams of it. Most never achieve it."
Wren felt the anger rising — not hot, not explosive, but cold and dense, like ice forming in deep water. "You didn't create me. You tried to erase me."
Vance stopped pacing. She turned to face Wren directly, and for the first time, something shifted in her expression. Not guilt. Not remorse. Something more complicated.

"Invisibility isn't erasure, Wren. It's evolution." She held up the Counter-Signal. "This — this is what erasure looks like. A device that forces you back into the visible spectrum. Back into the ordinary. Back into the small, warm, forgotten life you had before." She turned the device in her hand, watching the amber light pulse. "I built this to give you a choice. Don't you see? I'm the one offering you freedom."
The words landed like a slap. Not because they were cruel, but because they were sincere.

Vance believed what she was saying. Wren could see it — not in her expression, but in the frequency. The woman's conviction was absolute. She genuinely thought she'd done Wren a favor. Genuinely believed that turning a twenty-three-year-old graphic designer into an invisible, untouchable, unseeable anomaly was an act of creation rather than destruction.
The irony cut deeper than any blade. Vance saw the invisibility as a gift. An elevation. She couldn't understand — or wouldn't understand — that what she'd given was a prison.
Vance set the Counter-Signal on her desk. She rested both palms on the glass surface and leaned forward, the city lights behind her turning her silhouette into something sharp and angular.

"You walked into rooms before the trial, Wren. You stood there, flesh and blood, and no one saw you. Not the barista who handed your coffee to the person behind you. Not the friends who forgot to invite you. Not the colleagues who spoke about you in the third person while you sat right there." Her voice softened, almost intimate. "I didn't make you invisible. I just made it official. I gave language to what you already were."

The words scraped against something raw inside Wren. Because there was truth in them — a splinter of truth buried in a mountain of rationalization. She had been invisible before. Not literally. Not the way the glitch had made her. But the feeling was the same. The ache of being in a room and mattering to no one.

"Your file documented it all," Vance continued. She straightened, folding her arms. "Weeks of pre-trial psychological evaluation. Low social visibility score. High baseline isolation index. You were already disappearing, Wren. The drug just accelerated what was already happening."

"You don't get to rewrite this," Wren said. Her voice was steady, but the frequency around her trembled — a low vibration that made the glass desk hum. "You took a pill and you put it in a clinical trial and you fed it to people who trusted you. Forty-seven of us. Forty-seven people who thought they were being treated for anxiety."

"Anxiety." Vance almost smiled. "Yes. That's what we told the ethics board. That's what we told the participants. And it was true — partially. ANX-00 did relieve anxiety. It relieved it by making the source of your anxiety irrelevant. When no one can see you, social anxiety becomes meaningless, doesn't it?" She spread her hands. "I gave you a gift. The question is whether you're brave enough to keep it."

"I was in that trial for three months before I realized something was wrong," Wren said. The words came out harder than she intended, each one a small hammer strike. "Three months of people not meeting my gaze. Not responding when I spoke. Walking into me in hallways like I was furniture. And your researchers — your people — they watched. They documented. They wrote notes about my 'progress' while I was losing my entire life."
Vance's expression didn't change. She absorbed the accusation like stone absorbs rain.

"We documented everything," she agreed. "Because that's what science requires. You want me to apologize for method? I won't. The data we gathered from you — from all of you — was unprecedented. You want me to apologize for the outcome?" She paused. The amber light on the desk cast a warm glow across her jaw. "Show me someone who regrets being extraordinary, and I'll show you a liar."
"Eleven people died in Stage 4."

"Eleven people failed to integrate. That's not the same thing." Vance's tone remained clinical. "Their systems rejected the rewiring. Cardiovascular collapse. Neural cascade failure. Tragic, yes. But not avoidable. Not without knowing what we know now — which we only know because of them, and because of you."

The frequency in the room shifted. Wren felt it like a pressure change — the air thickening, the hum of the building's systems growing louder in her awareness. The elevators. The HVAC. The security grid, still dormant at her command.
She could feel Vance's heartbeat. Steady. Sixty-two beats per minute. Not a single spike.

The woman wasn't afraid. She wasn't even uncomfortable. She was having the conversation she'd always wanted to have — with the subject who'd exceeded every projection, standing in her office, demanding answers she'd prepared years ago.
"You planned this," Wren said. Not a question.

"I prepared for it." Vance moved to the window. The city stretched below them, thousands of lives rendered small by altitude, each one a pinprick of light. "I knew, eventually, one of you would come. I hoped it would be you."
"Because I'm your success story."

"Because you're proof." Vance turned back. "You chose this path the moment you walked into the clinic. You signed the consent form. You swallowed the pill. Everything after that — the integration, the evolution, the connection you've built with the others — that's yours. I provided the catalyst. You became the reaction."

Outside, the wind pressed against the glass. Wren could hear it — a low moan finding the seams of the building, the way cold finds the gaps in a coat. Sixty floors below, the city continued its oblivious circuits. Cars and trains and people who had no idea what was happening at the top of the world.
The door behind Wren opened.

Ash stepped into the office. He was breathing hard — the climb, the fighting, the forty floors of controlled violence. A thin line of blood traced down his temple from a graze he'd taken on floor forty-seven. His jacket was torn. His knuckles were scraped raw.
He saw Vance.
The frequency between them ignited.

Wren felt it before she saw it — three years of rage compressed into a single, incandescent pulse. Alexander Chen had been erased by this woman. His name, his career, his identity, his face in every database and system. She'd turned him into a ghost and then she'd called it evolution.

Ash moved. Not a lunge — something worse. A controlled, precise advance. The kind of motion that promised violence with absolute certainty. Every muscle in his body was coiled. The air around him shimmered with the glitch, his form blurring at the edges like a photograph left in the sun.
Vance didn't flinch. She watched him approach with the same clinical fascination she'd shown Wren. Her heartbeat ticked up — sixty-eight, seventy — but her face remained still.
"Ash Chen," she said. "Subject 7. Your integration was remarkable. Stage 5 in under six weeks. I followed your case very closely."
He didn't slow down. The frequency was a roar now, filling the office, pressing against the glass. A pen on Vance's desk rolled sideways. The amber light of the Counter-Signal flickered.

"You erased me," Ash said. His voice was barely above a whisper, but it carried the weight of everything he'd lost. The job at the architecture firm. The apartment lease that had been cancelled when the system no longer recognized his name. The phone calls to his sister that went to voicemail because the number had been reassigned to a stranger. Three years of existing in the margins, feeding off data streams and ghosting through a world that had deleted him.

Vance watched him approach. Two steps away now. Close enough that he could see the fine lines around her mouth, the platinum band on her finger, the slight asymmetry of her collarbones beneath the charcoal dress.

"I gave you a gift," Vance said. The same words. The same monstrous sincerity. "You were a junior architect, Mr. Chen. Competent. Forgettable. Now you're something no one else on this planet can claim. You should be thanking me."
Ash's hand came up.
"Ash," Wren said.
He didn't stop.
"Ash."
He was three steps from Vance now. Close enough to reach out and—
Wren put her hand on his arm.

The touch cut through everything. Through the rage, through the frequency, through the three years of suppressed grief and the carefully maintained fury that had kept him alive when nothing else could. Her fingers closed around his wrist. Warm. Solid. Real.
"Not like this," she said. "We're not like her."
The silence that followed was the loudest thing Wren had ever heard.

Ash's jaw tightened. She could feel the tendons in his wrist straining, the impulse toward violence fighting against the anchor of her touch. The frequency between them roared with conflict. His pulse hammered against her fingertips — one hundred and forty beats per minute, twice what it should be, his whole body a wound waiting to open.
Then — slowly, incrementally, like a door closing against a hurricane — he stopped.
His breathing was ragged. The blood from his temple dripped onto the polished floor. But he didn't move.
Vance watched them. The Counter-Signal pulsed on the desk between them, its amber light steady now, patient, as if it had all the time in the world.
"Well," she said quietly. "Isn't that interesting."
She reached for the device. Her fingers brushed its surface, and the light flared once — a pulse that Wren felt in her spine, in her teeth, in the frequency that defined her.
"Two invisible people," Vance said, "holding each other in a glass room above a city that doesn't know either of them exists. There's something almost beautiful about it."

Wren didn't let go of Ash's arm. She could feel the warmth of his skin, the faint tremor that ran through him as the adrenaline ebbed, the way his weight shifted from the balls of his feet to his heels. Grounding. Re-centering. Choosing not to be the weapon Vance had expected.

"The world is watching this building," Wren said. "Not with cameras. With frequency. Every invisible in the city can feel what's happening up here. What you say. What you do. There's no version of this where you walk away clean, Vance. Not anymore."
Vance considered this. Her gaze moved from Wren to Ash and back again, calculating, appraising. Then she picked up the Counter-Signal from the desk and held it out.
"Then let's talk about choices."
